A design pattern captures a recurring structure and the intent behind it. Memorizing UML is useless; explaining WHY you reached for Factory vs new Foo() is what matters.

Focus patterns for backends (GFG shortlist + daily Spring usage):
• Singleton — one shared instance (careful with state)
• Factory — decouple creation
• Decorator — add behavior without rewriting the core
• Observer — react to events

Also know Strategy (swap algorithms) — you already use it for payments.

Intent: exactly one instance, global access.

Spring: @Component/@Service beans are singleton by default — that is usually what you want for stateless services.

Dangers:
• Mutable fields on singletons = request crosstalk under concurrency
• Classic double-checked locking bugs (pre-modern Java)
• Hard to test if accessed via static getInstance()

Prefer dependency injection over static singletons. Treat “singleton” as a lifecycle, not a pattern to force everywhere.

Intent: let callers ask for a product without knowing concrete classes.

Examples:
• PaymentFactory.create("bkash") → BkashPayment
• ObjectMapper / HttpClient builders
• Spring BeanFactory / @Configuration @Bean methods

Use when creation logic is non-trivial (config, env, feature flags) or when you need to swap implementations.

Intent: wrap an object to add behavior while keeping the same interface (Open/Closed).

Examples:
• Java I/O: new BufferedInputStream(new FileInputStream(...))
• Spring: DataSource wrapping for metrics, caching decorator around a repository
• HTTP middleware / servlet filters chain

Decorator vs inheritance: wrapping composes at runtime; deep inheritance freezes hierarchy.

Intent: dependents get notified when a subject changes.

In-process: Spring ApplicationEventPublisher + @EventListener.
Across services: message broker topics (Kafka) — Observer at distributed scale.

Rules:
• Keep listeners fast or hand off to async executors
• Do not assume ordering unless the bus guarantees it
• Make handlers idempotent if events can replay

Need one shared stateless service? Singleton lifecycle via DI.
Need to construct variants? Factory/Strategy.
Need cross-cutting add-ons (logging, retry, metrics)? Decorator/middleware.
Need decoupled reactions? Observer/events.

Anti-pattern: Pattern zoo — three Factories wrapping two Singletons for a simple CRUD.

tips: Name the intent first, then the pattern — interviewers listen for intent.
Spring singletons must stay stateless or use thread-safe structures.
Decorator chains are middleware; keep each layer focused.
Distributed Observer = messaging; assume at-least-once delivery.

quiz.question: Decorator primarily supports which SOLID principle?
quiz.explanation: Extend behavior without modifying the original class.

quiz.question: Spring @Service default scope is…
quiz.explanation: One shared bean instance per container.

quiz.question: Factory is best when…
options: Creation logic/variants should be decoupled from callers
quiz.explanation: Callers depend on abstractions, not concretes.

quiz.question: Observer across microservices is usually…
quiz.explanation: Brokers realize distributed pub/sub.

quiz.question: Danger of mutable singleton service fields?
quiz.explanation: Shared mutable state under concurrency.

problems.title: Pick a pattern
problems.prompt: You need to add metrics timing around an existing UserRepository without editing its source. Which pattern?
problems.solution: Decorator: Caching/MetricsUserRepository implements UserRepository and delegates.

problems.title: Observer pitfall
problems.prompt: An @EventListener sends email synchronously inside the request thread. What goes wrong and how do you fix?
problems.solution: Email outages/latency block checkout. Publish event and process async (@Async or message queue) with retries/idempotency.

title: Caching
tag: Redis, TTLs, invalidation — speed without lying
connections: Databases (cache is not source of truth)
System Design (hot-key and stampede)
Idempotency (cached responses for POSTs need care)

sections.b:
Caching stores expensive results closer to the caller: CPU L1, process memory, Redis, CDN. Goal: lower latency and protect the database.

Broken caches cause:
• Stale reads (users see old prices)
• Cache stampede / thundering herd when a hot key expires
• Cache as source of truth (data loss on flush)
• Giant values / no TTL → OOM

Always know: what is authoritative? how fresh must it be? what is the invalidation story?

Cache-aside (lazy): app reads cache; on miss load DB and populate. On write, update DB then invalidate (or update) cache.
Write-through: write DB and cache together in the write path.
Write-behind: write cache first, flush DB async — dangerous for money.

Default for most CRUD APIs: cache-aside + invalidate on write.

TTL: soft bound on staleness. Short TTL for volatile data; longer for reference data.
Eviction: LRU/LFU when memory pressure hits — size your Redis.
Key design: namespace:entity:id:version — e.g. user:v2:42. Include version when schema changes.
Never store secrets in shared caches without encryption + access control.

When a popular key expires, 1,000 requests miss and stampede the DB.

Mitigations:
• Singleflight / request coalescing — one loader, others wait
• Probabilistic early expiration / soft TTL refresh
• Lock around populate (SETNX) with short lock TTL
• Stale-while-revalidate: serve stale briefly while refreshing

Measure: DB QPS spikes aligned with TTL boundaries.

Hardest problem in CS (joke with truth).

Patterns:
• Delete key on write (simple, brief miss storm possible)
• Versioned keys: bump version so old keys become unreachable
• Pub/sub invalidation across nodes for local Caffeine caches
• Time-based only when occasional staleness is acceptable

Document freshness SLOs per endpoint ("product name may lag 30s").

Use Redis for: session store, rate limits, hot entity cache, distributed locks (carefully), leaderboards (ZSET).

Do not use Redis as your only database for durable business records.

Observability: hit ratio, eviction rate, memory, p99 get latency, stampede indicators.

tips: Invalidate-on-write beats hoping TTL is “good enough” for user-edited data.
Always bound memory and set TTLs — caches without eviction policies become outages.
Stampede control is mandatory for hot keys.
Cache hit ratio without latency/error context can mislead — watch the DB too.

quiz.question: Cache-aside miss path should…
quiz.explanation: Lazy populate from source of truth.

quiz.question: Thundering herd happens when…
options: Many clients miss the same expired hot key together
quiz.explanation: Synchronized expiry storms the DB.

quiz.question: Safest default after a write?
quiz.explanation: Delete or version the key.

quiz.question: Redis as sole store for bank balances?
options: No — not durable source of truth for critical records
quiz.explanation: Use a real database for durable money state.

quiz.question: Singleflight/coalescing helps by…
options: Allowing only one loader for a miss; others wait
quiz.explanation: Collapse duplicate loads.

problems.title: Choose TTL
problems.prompt: Product catalog changes rarely; prices change often. How do you cache?
problems.solution: Split keys: catalog:product:id long TTL; price:product:id short TTL or invalidate on price write.

problems.title: Stampede design
problems.prompt: Hot key leaderboard expires every 60s and DB spikes. Propose two mitigations.
problems.solution: Use SETNX singleflight populate; or serve stale for 10s while refreshing; jitter TTLs.

title: Streaming & Messaging
tag: Kafka, outbox, DLQ — async that survives failure
connections: Idempotency (at-least-once requires it)
System Design (decouple producers/consumers)
Observability (consumer lag)

sections.b:
Synchronous chains amplify latency and failure. Queues/streams decouple producers from consumers, absorb spikes, and enable multiple independent consumers.

Use messaging for: emails, webhooks, search indexing, fan-out, workflow steps, event-driven integration.

Do not use messaging to avoid designing an API — it adds operational complexity (ordering, duplicates, poison messages).

Topics + partitions. Partition key chooses ordering scope (e.g. orderId). Consumers in a group split partitions.

Guarantees commonly relied on:
• Durable log, replayable
• At-least-once delivery to consumers (duplicates happen)
• Ordering per partition, not global

Producer idempotence (enable.idempotence=true, acks=all) prevents duplicate writes into Kafka — it does NOT make your DB side effects exactly-once.

Dual-write bug: update orders table, then publish to Kafka — process crashes between → lost event (or the opposite ordering publishes an event for rolled-back work).

Outbox pattern:
1) In one DB transaction: write business row + outbox row
2) Relay process publishes outbox → Kafka and marks published
3) Consumers process idempotently

This is the standard integrity pattern for event-driven microservices.

Assume every message can arrive twice.

Consumer pattern:
• Derive stable eventId
• Begin DB transaction; insert into processed_events(event_id) unique; if conflict, skip
• Apply business effect; commit

Poison messages: after N failures, push to Dead Letter Queue for inspection — do not block the partition forever. Alert on DLQ depth.

RabbitMQ: flexible routing (exchanges), classic work queues, ack per message — great for task distribution.
Kafka: high-throughput log, replay, event streaming, many independent consumer groups.

Choose Kafka when you need replay/fan-out history; choose Rabbit when you need complex routing and classic workers. Either way: idempotency + observability.

• Consumer lag (Kafka) — are you falling behind?
• Publish failure rate / relay lag (outbox age)
• DLQ count
• Handler p99 duration
• Partition hot-key skew

Lag growing under stable traffic = under-provisioned consumers or stuck handler.

tips: Kafka producer idempotence ≠ exactly-once business effects — still dedupe in the consumer/DB.
Outbox is mandatory when DB + event must not diverge.
Alert on consumer lag and DLQ depth, not only error logs.
Pick partition keys for the ordering you actually need.

quiz.question: At-least-once delivery implies…
quiz.explanation: Retries create duplicates.

quiz.question: Transactional Outbox solves…
options: Dual-write loss between DB and broker
quiz.explanation: Atomic business + event record in DB.

quiz.question: Kafka ordering is guaranteed…
quiz.explanation: Order within a partition.

quiz.question: DLQ is for…
quiz.explanation: Isolate bad messages.

quiz.question: enable.idempotence=true mainly prevents…
options: Duplicate records inside Kafka from producer retries
quiz.explanation: Producer-level Kafka duplicates, not app side effects.

problems.title: Dual-write failure
problems.prompt: Service saves Payment then publish PaymentCompleted; crash between. What is lost and how do you fix?
problems.solution: Event never published → downstream never runs. Write outbox in same DB tx; relay publishes.

problems.title: Idempotent consumer
problems.prompt: Sketch DB-backed dedupe for event handlers.
problems.solution: processed_events(event_id PK); insert first; on conflict skip; else apply effects in same transaction when possible.

title: Idempotency
tag: Safe retries — same request, same effect
connections: APIs (Idempotency-Key)
Messaging (duplicate deliveries)
Databases (unique constraints as guards)

sections.h: Definition and why networks force it
sections.b:
An operation is idempotent if performing it once or many times has the same effect as performing it once.

In distributed systems, timeouts are ambiguous: the server may have succeeded while the client saw failure. Clients retry. Without idempotency you get double charges, duplicate orders, and double emails.

Reliability requires retries; retries require idempotency.

GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent by spec intent (PUT replaces the same resource).
POST is not idempotent by default — creating a payment twice creates two payments.

Make POST safe with Idempotency-Key (Stripe-style):
• Client sends unique key per logical action
• Server stores key → response/result
• Retries with same key return the original result without re-executing side effects

1) Require Idempotency-Key on sensitive POSTs
2) Begin transaction
3) INSERT into idempotency_keys(key, request_hash, status, response) — unique(key)
4) If duplicate key with same hash: return stored response
5) If duplicate with different hash: 409 conflict
6) Else perform work, store response, commit

Include request hash so a reused key with a different body cannot silently do the wrong thing.
Expire keys after a retention window (e.g. 24h) with a TTL job.

Brokers give at-least-once. Exactly-once effect is an application property:
• Stable event identity
• Dedupe table or upsert natural key
• Side effects inside the same transaction as the dedupe insert when possible

Kafka EOS helps Kafka-internal pipelines; once you touch an external DB/API, you still design idempotent handlers.

Payments: key = wallet-tx-uuid from client.
Order create: key per checkout attempt.
Webhook handlers: use provider event id as dedupe key.
Email: store notification already sent for order X type Y.

Anti-pattern: dedupe only in Redis without DB uniqueness — Redis flush re-enables duplicates.

Replay the same request 10 times — assert one row, one charge, same response body/code.
Chaos: kill the server between commit and response; client retries; still one effect.
For consumers: deliver the same Kafka record twice in a test harness.

tips: Treat every timeout as maybe succeeded — design retries accordingly.
Persist idempotency records in the same DB as the side effect when you can.
Hash the request body with the key to prevent key reuse mistakes.
Idempotency is a product feature for payments, not an afterthought.

quiz.question: POST is made idempotent in practice with…
quiz.explanation: Client key + persisted result.

quiz.question: At-least-once delivery requires consumers to…
quiz.explanation: Duplicates are normal.

quiz.question: Same Idempotency-Key, different body should…
quiz.explanation: 409 or equivalent.

quiz.question: Best place for durable dedupe of payments?
quiz.explanation: Durable shared store.

quiz.question: Timeout then retry without idempotency risks…
quiz.explanation: Ambiguous success + second execution.

problems.title: Payment API
problems.prompt: Design headers/tables for idempotent POST /charges.
problems.solution: Idempotency-Key header; table(key PK, body_hash, status_code, response_json, created_at); first writer executes charge.

problems.title: Webhook replay
problems.prompt: Provider retries webhooks. How do you avoid double fulfillment?
problems.solution: Store provider event_id uniquely; process only on first insert.

title: SSL, JWT & OAuth
tag: TLS, tokens, delegated auth — production patterns
connections: Security (defense in depth)
APIs (Bearer tokens)
Cloud (certs, secrets, gateways)

sections.b:
TLS (successor of SSL) provides confidentiality and integrity for data in transit. HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.

Without TLS, bearer tokens and passwords can be sniffed. OAuth and JWT do NOT encrypt the channel by themselves — HTTPS is mandatory in production.

Also know: certificate chains, expiry, HSTS, and terminating TLS at a load balancer vs mTLS between services.

JWT: header.payload.signature (base64url). Common claims: sub, iss, aud, exp, iat.

Production rules (2026):
• Prefer RS256/ES256 with JWKS over shared HS256 across many services
• Short-lived access tokens (minutes) + refresh token rotation
• Validate exp/iss/aud/signature; reject alg=none
• Do not stuff sensitive PII into tokens
• JWTs are hard to revoke — keep them short or use denylist/introspection for high-risk cases

Authorization Code + PKCE: SPAs/mobile/user login (delegated access).
Client Credentials: service-to-service, no user present.
Refresh tokens: obtain new access tokens; rotate and detect reuse.

Resource Server (your API) validates access tokens. Authorization Server (Keycloak/Auth0/Cognito) issues them.

Mutual TLS: both sides present certificates. Proves service identity on the network, complementary to user JWTs.

At scale, service mesh (Istio) automates short-lived certs. App still checks user JWT for user-level authorization.

Defense in depth: mTLS (service) + JWT (user) + RBAC on the action.

API as OAuth2 resource server: validate JWT via issuer-uri / jwk-set-uri.
Separate authentication (401) from authorization (403).
Protect /token endpoints with rate limits.
Store client secrets in a secret manager — never in git.

• HS256 secret leaked from any microservice → forge tokens for all
• Eternal JWTs with no exp
• Accepting tokens without aud check (token confusion)
• CORS + cookies misconfig leading to token theft
• Logging Authorization headers

code:
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: https://auth.example.com/realms/app

// Validate: signature via JWKS, exp, iss, aud, scopes
tips: HTTPS first — tokens on cleartext HTTP are already compromised.
RS256 + JWKS scales safer than a shared HS256 secret.
Short access token TTL + rotating refresh tokens.
401 unauthenticated vs 403 unauthorized — use both correctly.

quiz.question: OAuth2/JWT without HTTPS is…
options: Required by JWT spec to skip TLS
quiz.explanation: TLS protects the channel.

quiz.question: Prefer RS256/JWKS over HS256 in microservices because…
options: Public verify keys can be shared; private signing key stays in the issuer
quiz.explanation: Avoid distributing a symmetric secret everywhere.

quiz.question: Authorization Code + PKCE is for…
quiz.explanation: Delegated user auth.

quiz.question: Client Credentials grant is for…
quiz.explanation: No user interaction.

quiz.question: mTLS proves…
options: Service identity via certificates on both sides
quiz.explanation: Mutual certificate auth.

problems.title: Token design
problems.prompt: List 5 validations your API must perform on an access JWT.
problems.solution: Verify signature with JWKS; check exp; iss; aud; required scopes/roles for the route.

problems.title: Flow choice
problems.prompt: Internal cron calls billing API with no user. Which OAuth flow?
problems.solution: Client Credentials grant (or mTLS + workload identity).

title: TDD — Test-Driven Development
tag: Red → green → refactor — design with tests
connections: Testing (pyramid, doubles)
Clean design (TDD pushes smaller units)
CI (tests gate merges)

sections.b:
TDD: write a failing test (red), write the minimal code to pass (green), improve design (refactor) while tests stay green.

TDD is not write tests after if you have time. It is a design feedback loop. It is also not 100% coverage theater — you still pick meaningful behaviors.

Use TDD heavily for domain logic and pure transforms; use broader tests for framework wiring.

1) Name a behavior: reject negative payment amounts
2) Write a test that fails for the right reason
3) Implement the simplest fix
4) Refactor names/structure
5) Next behavior

Keep tests in AAA form: Arrange, Act, Assert. One logical assertion theme per test.

Stub: returns canned data.
Mock: verifies interactions.
Fake: in-memory repo.

Over-mocking leads to brittle tests that mirror implementation. Prefer fakes for repositories when exercising domain services; mock only awkward boundaries (payment gateway, clock, random).

Inside-out: start from domain units.
Outside-in (ATDD): start from an acceptance test, discover collaborators.

For APIs: one acceptance test for the happy path + focused unit TDD for rules (discounts, eligibility).

Cannot TDD a ball of mud instantly. Add characterization tests that lock current behavior, then refactor under that safety net, then grow TDD for new features.

Seams: extract pure functions, introduce interfaces at I/O boundaries.

Tests must be deterministic and fast. Flakes destroy trust.
TDD works when the team protects the red-green-refactor rhythm in PRs — not when coverage badges replace thinking.

tips: Fail for the right reason — a wrong compile error is not a good red.
Refactor only on green; never mix refactor with new behavior.
Prefer fakes over heavy mocks for repositories.
Characterization tests unlock legacy refactors safely.

quiz.question: Correct TDD order?
quiz.explanation: Classic cycle.

quiz.question: A fake repository is useful because…
options: It gives fast deterministic storage for unit/domain tests
quiz.explanation: In-memory seam.

quiz.question: Over-mocking often causes…
quiz.explanation: Tests break on harmless refactors.

quiz.question: Characterization tests…
quiz.explanation: Safety net on existing behavior.

quiz.question: AAA means…
quiz.explanation: Structure every test.

problems.title: Write the red test
problems.prompt: For discount: orders >= 100 get 10% off. Write a test name + assert sketch before implementation.
problems.solution: @Test void appliesTenPercentWhenTotalAtLeast100() { assertEquals(90, pricing.total(cartWith(100))); }

problems.title: Choose double
problems.prompt: PaymentService talks to Stripe HTTP. What do you use in unit TDD and why?
problems.solution: Mock/fake PaymentGateway interface — do not call Stripe in unit tests; keep an integration test separate.

title: Networking Fundamentals
tag: DNS → TCP → TLS → HTTP — the path of a request
connections: APIs (HTTP semantics)
Auth/TLS (HTTPS)
System Design (LB, CDN, latency)

sections.h: The journey of a browser request
sections.b:
1) DNS resolves api.example.com → IP
2) TCP handshake to port 443
3) TLS handshake
4) HTTP request
5) Load balancer → service → DB
6) Response reverse path

Debugging starts here: which hop failed?

TCP: reliable, ordered, congestion-controlled — HTTP, databases, most APIs.
UDP: no connection guarantee — video, gaming, DNS often uses UDP.

HTTP/3 uses QUIC (UDP-based) but restores reliability in userspace.

HTTP/1.1: persistent connections.
HTTP/2: multiplexing over one TCP connection.
Keep-alive and connection pools matter for service-to-service calls.

Timeouts: connect vs read vs overall. Retries only when idempotent.

L4 vs L7 load balancing. Reverse proxies terminate TLS, route by path/host.
CDN: edge caches for static assets.

Know X-Forwarded-For trust boundaries and health checks.

• dig/nslookup for DNS
• curl -v for TLS/HTTP headers
• Distinguish client timeout vs server 5xx vs LB 502

code:
dig api.example.com
curl -v https://api.example.com/health

HttpClient.newBuilder()
  .connectTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(2))
  .build();
tips: Separate DNS, TCP, TLS, and app-layer failures when debugging.
Set aggressive connect timeouts; bound total retries.
Know whether your LB health check hits /health correctly.
CDN caches are part of your system — purge strategy matters.

quiz.question: HTTPS is…
quiz.explanation: TLS-wrapped HTTP.

quiz.question: TCP provides…
quiz.explanation: Connection-oriented reliability.

quiz.question: DNS purpose?
quiz.explanation: Name resolution.

quiz.question: Safe automatic retries are for…
quiz.explanation: Avoid double side effects.

quiz.question: 502 from load balancer often means…
quiz.explanation: Gateway cannot get a valid upstream response.

problems.title: Timeout design
problems.prompt: Service A calls B. Pick timeout/retry policy.
problems.solution: Short connect timeout; overall deadline; retry only idempotent GETs/PUTs with jittered backoff.

problems.title: Map the hops
problems.prompt: List hops from mobile app to Spring on K8s behind Cloudflare.
problems.solution: DNS → CDN/edge → LB → ingress → service → pod; TLS may terminate at edge/ingress.

title: Operating Systems Basics
tag: Processes, memory, I/O — enough to reason about backends
connections: Concurrency (threads schedule on CPUs)
Containers (cgroups/limits)
Debugging (CPU, RSS, file descriptors)

sections.b:
Process: isolated address space, heavy to create.
Thread: shares memory inside a process — cheap communication, easy races.

Backends are multi-threaded processes. Containers isolate processes with cgroups/namespaces.

Runnable vs blocked (waiting on I/O/lock).
High latency + low CPU often means blocking/waiting, not compute starvation.

Stack vs heap. GC languages: allocation churn → GC pauses.
RSS vs heap metrics. OOMKilled in Kubernetes = limit hit.
Bound caches or you leak.

Everything is a file descriptor on Unix: files, sockets, pipes.
Too many open connections → FD exhaustion. Tune ulimit; fix leaks; pool clients.
Connection pools, thread pools, container CPU throttling, page cache vs disk I/O — OS fundamentals explain production incidents.

code:
ps aux | grep java
ls -l /proc/<pid>/fd | wc -l
free -h
ulimit -n
tips: Latency + low CPU → look for blocking I/O or lock waits.
Always bound caches and pools — OS will OOM you otherwise.
FD leaks look like random connection failures under load.
Container limits change what enough CPU means — watch throttling.

quiz.question: Threads in one process share…
quiz.explanation: Shared memory → races possible.

quiz.question: OOMKilled usually means…
quiz.explanation: cgroup memory limit.

quiz.question: Too many open files points to…
quiz.explanation: Socket/file descriptor leak or limit too low.

quiz.question: Blocked thread is typically…
options: Waiting on I/O or a lock
quiz.explanation: Not runnable until event arrives.

quiz.question: Process isolation means…
quiz.explanation: OS process boundaries.

problems.title: Read the symptoms
problems.prompt: p99=2s, CPU=10%, DB pool waits high. What is happening?
problems.solution: Threads blocked waiting for DB connections — not CPU bound; fix pool/query/concurrency.

problems.title: Limits
problems.prompt: Why set memory limits on a JVM pod?
problems.solution: Contain blast radius; surface leaks via OOMKilled; size heap below limit.

title: GraphQL & gRPC
tag: When REST is not enough — schemas, N+1, protobuf
connections: APIs (REST comparison)
Networking (HTTP/2 for gRPC)
Caching (GraphQL cache complexity)

sections.h: REST is not the only API style
sections.b:
REST/JSON over HTTP is the default public API. Internally and for specialized clients, GraphQL and gRPC are common.

Choose based on clients, coupling, tooling, and performance — not fashion.

Schema defines types and queries/mutations/subscriptions.
Client asks for exactly the fields it needs.

Costs: complex authz, harder caching, N+1 in resolvers (DataLoader), need query cost limits.

Protobuf contracts, HTTP/2, codegen stubs. Excellent for service-to-service: low latency, strong typing, streaming RPCs.

Browser clients usually need grpc-web or a gateway.

Public multi-language clients → REST.
Many mobile screens needing flexible fields → GraphQL with discipline.
Internal microservice mesh / streaming → gRPC.

Mix: REST edge + gRPC interior.

GraphQL: unbounded queries as DoS — persist allowlists or cost analysis.
gRPC: version protobuf carefully; never reuse field numbers incorrectly.
Both still need authn/z, timeouts, observability, idempotency for writes.

tips: GraphQL without DataLoader often recreates N+1.
gRPC shines inside the data center; REST still wins many public APIs.
Limit GraphQL query cost — treat queries like untrusted code.
Contracts (OpenAPI/protobuf/GraphQL schema) are part of API design.

quiz.question: GraphQL N+1 is fixed with…
quiz.explanation: Batch backend fetches.

quiz.question: gRPC commonly uses…
quiz.explanation: Modern RPC stack.

quiz.question: Best default for public CRUD HTTP API?
quiz.explanation: Ecosystem and simplicity.

quiz.question: GraphQL caching is harder because…
options: Queries vary by shape; URL cache keys less natural
quiz.explanation: Flexible queries complicate CDN/HTTP caching.

quiz.question: Mixed architecture pattern?
options: One giant GraphQL for DBs directly
quiz.explanation: Common pragmatic split.

problems.title: Choose API style
problems.prompt: Mobile app needs many screens with different fields from same backend. Options?
problems.solution: GraphQL or a BFF that shapes REST DTOs per screen.

problems.title: Secure GraphQL
problems.prompt: List 3 production controls for a public GraphQL endpoint.
problems.solution: AuthZ per field; max depth/complexity; persisted queries; rate limits; timeout.
